source_paper_title: Tightness Without Counterexamples: A New Approach and New Results for Prophet Inequalities
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Jiang_2025, title={Tightness Without Counterexamples: A New Approach and New Results for Prophet Inequalities}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0221}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0221}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Jiang, Jiashuo and Ma, Will and Zhang, Jiawei}, year={2025}, month=apr }
context:
Page 6, Introduction, item 2 under “1.2. New Results”.

It remains a challenging problem to analytically verify for all \(k > 1\) our numerical observation that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\), which would resolve the open question of k-unit IID prophet inequality.
title: Analytically prove equality of asymptotic and worst-case IID k-unit prophet ratios
background_latex:
Consider the IID multi-unit prophet inequality with a uniform matroid (capacity) constraint. There are integers \(k\ge 2\) (number of selection slots) and \(n\ge 1\) (number of sequentially arriving agents). Each arriving agent \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) reveals a nonnegative random value \(R_i\) upon arrival; the \(R_i\) are independent and identically distributed (IID) according to a known distribution. An online policy must irrevocably accept or reject each agent upon arrival, may accept at most \(k\) agents in total, and aims to maximize expected total accepted value \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{i\in S} R_i]\), where \(|S|\le k\) is the (random) accepted set.

Let \(\mathrm{DP}(I)\) denote the expected value of an optimal adaptive online policy (equivalently, the optimal dynamic program) on instance \(I\). Let \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) denote the prophet benchmark, defined as the expected sum of the \(k\) largest realized values among \(R_1,\dots,R_n\), i.e.
\[\mathrm{Proph}(I)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\max_{S\subseteq \{1,\dots,n\},\ |S|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in S} R_i\Big].\]
For fixed \(k\) and \(n\), define the tight IID prophet-inequality guarantee
\[\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)},\]
where \(\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}\) denotes the class of all IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents (over all nonnegative value distributions).

Two natural aggregations over \(n\) are:
1) the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n}\) (when it exists), and
2) the worst case over all horizons \(\inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\).

For \(k=1\), classical work characterizes \(\gamma_{1,n}\) and shows the worst case occurs as \(n\to\infty\). For \(k>1\), the paper reports numerical evidence about the relationship between \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{k,n}\), but does not give an analytic proof for all \(k>1\).
problem_definition_latex:
For each integer \(k>1\), establish analytically that
\[\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n},\]
where \(\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) is the tight performance guarantee of the optimal adaptive online policy relative to the prophet benchmark over IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents.
latex:
Consider the IID multi-unit prophet inequality with a uniform matroid (capacity) constraint. There are integers \(k\ge 2\) (number of selection slots) and \(n\ge 1\) (number of sequentially arriving agents). Each arriving agent \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) reveals a nonnegative random value \(R_i\) upon arrival; the \(R_i\) are independent and identically distributed (IID) according to a known distribution. An online policy must irrevocably accept or reject each agent upon arrival, may accept at most \(k\) agents in total, and aims to maximize expected total accepted value \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{i\in S} R_i]\), where \(|S|\le k\) is the (random) accepted set.

Let \(\mathrm{DP}(I)\) denote the expected value of an optimal adaptive online policy (equivalently, the optimal dynamic program) on instance \(I\). Let \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) denote the prophet benchmark, defined as the expected sum of the \(k\) largest realized values among \(R_1,\dots,R_n\), i.e.
\[\mathrm{Proph}(I)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\max_{S\subseteq \{1,\dots,n\},\ |S|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in S} R_i\Big].\]
For fixed \(k\) and \(n\), define the tight IID prophet-inequality guarantee
\[\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)},\]
where \(\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}\) denotes the class of all IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents (over all nonnegative value distributions).

Two natural aggregations over \(n\) are:
1) the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n}\) (when it exists), and

For \(k=1\), classical work characterizes \(\gamma_{1,n}\) and shows the worst case occurs as \(n\to\infty\). For \(k>1\), the paper reports numerical evidence about the relationship between \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{k,n}\), but does not give an analytic proof for all \(k>1\).

For each integer \(k>1\), establish analytically that
\[\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n},\]
where \(\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) is the tight performance guarantee of the optimal adaptive online policy relative to the prophet benchmark over IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents.
location_in_paper: Page 6, Introduction, item 2 under “1.2. New Results”.
open_problem_quote: It remains a challenging problem to analytically verify for all \(k > 1\) our numerical observation that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\), which would resolve the open question of k-unit IID prophet inequality.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is an equality between a limit and an infimum over all horizons and all IID distributions, so falsifying it would require searching over a very large (infinite-dimensional) space of value distributions and proving optimality gaps; finite instance searches give at best suggestive evidence.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can numerically approximate \(\gamma_{k,n}\) via the paper’s semi-infinite LP discretization for many \((k,n)\) and attempt to detect candidate worst-case horizons or distributions, providing strong experimental guidance. However, turning this into an analytic proof of the equality for all \(k>1\) likely requires new theoretical ideas beyond automated search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In the notation of Jiang–Ma–Zhang (2025), the IID capacity-\(k\) tight ratio \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{I\in\mathcal I^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) admits an exact minimax reformulation as a semi-infinite “type coverage” LP (their \(\mathrm{iidLP}^{\mathrm{DP/Proph}}_{k,n}\)), obtained by dualizing an inner LP over value gaps \(\Delta_j\) after fixing the quantile-type distribution. This framework yields numerical evidence (e.g. Table 1 for small \(k\)) and motivates the open question whether the horizon-worst case \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{k,n}\) is attained asymptotically, i.e. equals \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\), for every fixed \(k>1\).

The strongest forward progress is the nonlinear-systems approach of “Splitting guarantees for prophet inequalities via nonlinear systems”, which (i) gives an infinite-dimensional LP \([P]_{n,k}\) that exactly characterizes \(\gamma_{k,n}\) for each finite \(n,k\), and (ii) derives a coupled ODE system producing explicit asymptotic lower bounds of the form \(\gamma_{k,n}\ge (1-O(k\log^2 n/n))\sum_{j=1}^k\theta_j^\star\) for all sufficiently large \(n\). This substantially advances analytic control of the \(n\to\infty\) regime, but it does not establish any horizon monotonicity or an identity between \(\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) and the asymptotic constant; indeed it leaves open whether \(\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}=\sum_{j=1}^k\theta_j^\star\).

Related works provide partial templates and tools rather than a solution. “Prophet Inequalities: Competing with the Top \(\ell\) Items is Easy” proves a clean horizon-monotonicity phenomenon \(CR_\ell(2n)\le CR_\ell(n)\) in a \(k=1\) setting (with a different benchmark), suggesting that proving a suitable monotonicity/subadditivity property in \(n\) could be the key missing step for \(k>1\). Several papers on static pricing/thresholds and OCRS (e.g. “Static pricing for multi-unit prophet inequalities” and “Tight Guarantees for Multi-unit Prophet Inequalities and Online Stochastic Knapsack*”) rigorously identify Poisson or infinitesimal-splitting limits as worst cases in their respective models, reinforcing the conjecture that the minimax over horizons should be governed by an asymptotic (often Poisson-like) limit. However, none currently bridge these Poisson-limit extremality principles to the full-information optimal DP vs prophet benchmark in the IID \(k\)-unit setting, so the equality \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}\) remains open for \(k>1\).

sources.title: Splitting guarantees for prophet inequalities via nonlinear systems
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces an infinite-dimensional LP [P]_{n,k} that exactly characterizes the worst-case i.i.d. k-selection prophet ratio \(\gamma_{n,k}\) for each finite \(n,k\) (Theorem 1), and a coupled nonlinear ODE system (1)–(3) with parameters \(\theta_1^\star,\dots,\theta_k^\star\) such that for each fixed \(k\) there exists \(n_0(k)\) with \[\gamma_{n,k}\ge \Bigl(1-\tfrac{24k\ln^2 n}{n}\Bigr)\sum_{j=1}^k \theta_j^\star\quad\text{for all }n\ge n_0(k).\] (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to prove analytically that for each \(k>1\), \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper develops asymptotic lower bounds on \(\gamma_{n,k}\) via an ODE system and shows these bounds hold for all sufficiently large \(n\), but it does not establish that the worst-case over \(n\) is attained asymptotically, nor any monotonicity/limit-equality statement relating \(\inf_n\gamma_{n,k}\) to \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{n,k}\). In fact, it explicitly states that obtaining tight analytic formulas and extending results to all \(n\) remain open, and it leaves open whether \(\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{n,k}=\sum_{j=1}^k\theta_j^\star\). Thus it provides tools and partial asymptotic progress but not the requested equality.

sources.title: Prophet Inequalities: Competing with the Top ℓ Items is Easy
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves for the single-selection case (decision maker selects 1 item) and benchmark equal to the average of the top \(\ell\) order statistics that the worst-case i.i.d. competitive ratio \(CR_\ell:=\inf_{n\ge \ell}\inf_F \sup_\tau \mathbb E[X_\tau]/\mathbb E[\frac1\ell\sum_{i=1}^\ell X_{(i)}]\) equals the unique solution in \([0,1)\) of an integral equation (Theorem 1.1), and shows a horizon-monotonicity property \(CR_\ell(2n)\le CR_\ell(n)\) implying \(CR_\ell=\liminf_{n\to\infty} CR_\ell(n)\) (Lemma 3.1 and Eq. (3.1)). For the multi-selection extension (select \(k\) items) with the same top-\(\ell\)-average benchmark, it gives an asymptotic lower bound \(\liminf_{n\to\infty} CR_{k,\ell}(n)\ge \frac{CR_\ell}{k}\sum_{j=1}^k \prod_{t=1}^j \theta_{t,\ell}^{-1}\) where \(\theta_{t,\ell}\) are defined via a boundary-value ODE system (Theorem 1.3), but states it does not prove asymptotic tightness nor that the worst case is attained as \(n\to\infty\) for \(k>1\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, for the standard i.i.d. \(k\)-unit prophet inequality with prophet benchmark equal to the sum of the top \(k\) values, to prove analytically that the worst-case ratio over horizons equals the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper proves an analogous ‘worst case at large \(n\)’ statement only in the \(k=1\) selection setting (Lemma 3.1 gives \(CR_\ell(2n)\le CR_\ell(n)\), hence \(\inf_n CR_\ell(n)=\liminf_{n\to\infty} CR_\ell(n)\)). However, it does not establish the corresponding horizon equality for the true \(k\)-unit benchmark (top \(k\) sum) nor even for its own \(k\)-selection generalization \(CR_{k,\ell}(n)\); it explicitly notes the lack of a proof that \(CR_{k,\ell}=\liminf_{n\to\infty} CR_{k,\ell}(n)\) for \(k>1\). Thus it provides partial progress/related evidence and techniques (ODE limits, boundary-value formulations) but does not solve the stated open problem for \(k>1\).

sources.title: Posted Pricing and Competition in Large Markets
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies large-market (fixed distribution, \(n\to\infty\)) welfare guarantees for fixed posted prices and competition complexity. For the \(k\)-unit i.i.d. setting it defines \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)=\liminf_{n\to\infty}\sup_T \mathbb{E}(R^n_{k,T})/\sum_{j=1}^k \mathbb{E}(M_n^j)\). Under an extreme-value condition on \(F\):
- If \(F\) is Fr\'echet with parameter \(\alpha\), then \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)\ge \varphi_k(\alpha)\) where \(\varphi_k\) is an explicit optimization expression (Eq. (3)); in particular \(\mathrm{apx}_1(F)\ge 0.712\) and \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)\ge 1-1/\sqrt{2\pi k}\).
- If \(F\) is Gumbel or reversed Weibull, then \(\mathrm{apx}_k(F)=1\).
It also characterizes large-market competition complexity \(C(F)\) for the optimal dynamic pricing/prophet policy as \(C(F)=(1-\gamma)(\Gamma(1-\gamma))^{1/\gamma}\) where \(\gamma\) is the extreme-value index (Theorem 4).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an analytic proof that for the i.i.d. \(k\)-unit prophet ratio \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_I \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\), the worst case over horizons equals the asymptotic limit: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper does not address \(\gamma_{k,n}\) for the optimal dynamic program versus the prophet benchmark across all i.i.d. distributions and finite \(n\). Instead it analyzes a different ratio: fixed-price (single-threshold) welfare versus the prophet/optimal-offline benchmark, and it does so in a large-market regime with additional assumptions (extreme value condition) on \(F\). While it cites work on i.i.d. \(k\)-unit prophet inequalities and discusses large-\(n\) behavior, it neither proves monotonicity/tightness of \(\gamma_{k,n}\) in \(n\) nor identifies the finite-\(n\) worst case for the optimal adaptive policy. Thus it does not solve the stated equality, but it provides related asymptotic analysis tools (extreme-value/regular-variation methods) that could be relevant for studying \(n\to\infty\) limits.

sources.title: Competition Versus Complexity in Multiple-Selection Prophet Inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies competition complexity for the i.i.d. multi-unit prophet setting within single-threshold policies. It gives an exact closed form for the worst-case (over distributions) competitive ratio of the best single-threshold algorithm when the online algorithm sees a length-
m sequence but is benchmarked against a prophet with length-
n sequence: 
\[\gamma_{m,n,k}=\inf_F\sup_{q\in[0,1]} \frac{\mathrm{ALG}_{m,k}(F,q)}{\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)}=\frac{Q_{m,k}(k/n)}{k},\]
where \(Q_{m,k}(q)=\mathbb{E}[\min\{k,Y\}]\) for \(Y\sim\mathrm{Binomial}(m,q)\). In particular, the optimal quantile is \(q=k/n\), independent of \(m\). It also derives bounds on the (1-
\(\varepsilon\))-competition complexity \(\beta_k(\varepsilon)\): for \(k=1\), \(\beta_1(\varepsilon)=\ln(1/\varepsilon)\); for \(k>1\), \(\ln(1/\varepsilon)/k\le \beta_k(\varepsilon)\le 1+\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}+\sqrt{\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}}\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to analytically prove that for the optimal adaptive online policy (dynamic program) in the i.i.d. k-unit prophet inequality, the worst-case ratio over horizons equals the asymptotic-in-n limit: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}\), where \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_I \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\). This paper does not analyze \(\mathrm{DP}(I)\) vs \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) as a function of \(n\); instead it analyzes a different ratio \(\gamma_{m,n,k}\) for the restricted class of single-threshold algorithms with an enlarged sample size \(m\) benchmarked against a prophet with horizon \(n\). Thus it does not resolve the equality of asymptotic and worst-case horizons for \(\gamma_{k,n}\) (optimal DP). However, its infinite-dimensional LP/duality framework and explicit extremal constructions in quantile space may be adaptable as analytic tools for horizon-monotonicity/tightness questions in the full DP setting.

sources.title: Static pricing for multi-unit prophet inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies worst-case competitive ratios of a particular class of online policies (anonymous, non-adaptive, order-oblivious static threshold pricing) for the multi-unit prophet inequality with capacity k. It proves that choosing a price \(\pi\) that equalizes \(\delta_k(X_\pi)=\mu_k(X_\pi)\), where \(\delta_k(X)=\Pr[X\le k-1]\) and \(\mu_k(X)=\tfrac1k\mathbb{E}[\min\{X,k\}]\), guarantees welfare at least \(\varphi_k\) times the prophet benchmark, where \(\varphi_k\) is characterized by a Poisson worst case: letting \(X_\lambda\sim\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)\) and choosing \(\lambda_k\) such that \(\delta_k(X_{\lambda_k})=\mu_k(X_{\lambda_k})\), the competitive ratio is at least \(\varphi_k:=\delta_k(X_{\lambda_k})=\mu_k(X_{\lambda_k})\).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the *optimal adaptive* IID k-unit prophet ratio \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_I \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) and asks to prove that \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper does not analyze \(\gamma_{k,n}\) as a function of horizon \(n\) for the optimal adaptive policy, nor does it compare asymptotic vs worst-case-in-n behavior. Instead it gives a horizon-independent worst-case guarantee for a restricted policy class (static prices) in the general (not necessarily IID) setting, and characterizes worst-case instances via Poisson limits. The Poisson-limit/worst-case characterization may be conceptually related to asymptotic-in-n phenomena, but it does not establish the equality of \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and \(\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) for optimal adaptive policies.

sources.title: Tight Guarantees for Multi-unit Prophet Inequalities and Online Stochastic Knapsack∗
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper characterizes the tight LP-relative guarantee \(\gamma_k^*\) for the adversarial-order \(k\)-unit prophet inequality / \(k\)-unit OCRS problem: \(\gamma_k^* = \inf_{p: \sum_t p_t = k} \mathrm{LPOCRS}_k(p)\), and the infimum is attained in the limit by splitting arrivals into infinitesimal probabilities, yielding a Poisson-rate-\(k\) limit characterized by an ODE (Theorem 2). It also proves tight LP-relative guarantee \(1/(3+e^{-2})\) for knapsack OCRS (Theorems 3–4).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, for IID \(k\)-unit prophet inequalities with horizon \(n\), to prove analytically that the worst-case ratio over \(n\) equals the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\), where \(\gamma_{k,n}\) is defined using the optimal online dynamic program divided by the prophet benchmark (top-\(k\) order statistics). This citing paper studies a different model: adversarial (possibly heterogeneous) arrivals and compares online algorithms to an LP relaxation (via OCRS), not to the prophet benchmark, and not restricted to IID. It does show a related “worst case is Poisson/infinitesimal splitting” phenomenon for the OCRS/LP benchmark (Lemma 2, Theorem 2), but it does not address the IID prophet-benchmark ratios \(\gamma_{k,n}\) nor the equality between \(\inf_n\) and \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\) in that IID setting.

sources.title: The iid prophet inequality with limited flexibility
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies the single-choice IID prophet inequality under the restriction that the stopping rule uses at most \(k\) distinct thresholds over \(n\) arrivals (a \(k\)-dynamic policy). It gives an infinite-dimensional LP characterization of the optimal approximation \(\gamma^*_{n,k}\) achievable by such policies and derives bounds such as \(\gamma^*_{n,1}=1-(1-1/n)^n\), \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma^*_{n,2}\approx 0.70804\), and for large \(k\ll n\), \(\gamma^*_{n,k}\ge \bar\gamma\bigl(1-c\,(\log k/k + k^2/n)\bigr)\) where \(\bar\gamma\approx 0.745\) is the optimal fully-dynamic IID prophet ratio.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns multi-unit (capacity \(k\)) IID prophet inequalities with a uniform matroid constraint and asks to prove \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) for each fixed capacity \(k>1\), where \(\gamma_{k,n}\) is the tight ratio of the optimal adaptive online policy to the prophet’s top-\(k\) benchmark. This citing paper instead considers the single-unit (choose at most one) IID prophet problem, but restricts the policy class to use at most \(k\) thresholds (a different parameter \(k\) measuring pricing flexibility, not selection capacity). It does not analyze the multi-unit/top-\(k\) prophet benchmark nor the equality between asymptotic-in-\(n\) and worst-case-in-\(n\) ratios for capacity \(k\). At most, its infinite-dimensional LP/ODE methodology could be a tool inspiration for analyzing horizon dependence in other prophet-inequality variants.
